title: Company Structure, Project Manager Scorecards, and Financial Systems
summary: Erin Stetzer led an open Q&A session covering company organizational structure, project manager roles and scorecards, and financial management systems for custom home builders. Participants across different business scales discussed challenges around delegating ownership of projects, managing subcontractor billing, and building team culture. Practical frameworks around calendar discipline, draw management, and trade vetting were shared throughout.

teachings.explanation: A defined set of non-negotiables that clarifies exactly what a project manager is responsible for, used both as a hiring tool and an ongoing accountability standard. The scorecard requires PMs to know the project better than anyone, execute weekly reports and daily videos, and uphold company standards consistently. It enables owners to step back from micromanaging individual projects.
teachings.quote: The ownership part is I'll break down our non-negotiables. So for our project managers, they have to know more than architects, more than designers, more than trades, more than anyone about the project.

teachings.concept: Daily Video Updates as a Delegation Tool
teachings.explanation: Project managers record multiple short videos per day per project, allowing the owner to stay informed and catch potential issues without being physically present. This bridges the gap between delegation and oversight, keeping owners in the game without requiring their constant on-site presence.
teachings.quote: They need to very specifically create three videos for me, hopefully a day per project, because what it does is like, I'm like you, I want to be in the projects. I just can't, I can't be there all the time, but I want to know what they're thinking.

teachings.explanation: Owners and project managers must own their own calendars by setting fixed, predictable times to be at each project site rather than reacting to everyone else's schedules. This reduces chaos, sets expectations for trades and clients, and prevents the feeling that everything in construction is an emergency.
teachings.quote: My entire calendar was every minute filled out to where I was going to be for that entire week. Repeat, repeat, repeat. I said, if anybody needs to talk to me, you will find me at that project at that time. I was not catering to everybody else's schedules.

teachings.concept: Approved Invoice Flow and Daily Draw Management
teachings.explanation: A structured daily process where invoices come in, are reviewed and approved or held by the project manager, then moved to an approved file from which the money manager assembles draws. This prevents unbilled or unapproved charges from slipping through and maintains financial clarity on every project.
teachings.quote: Not once a week, not every other week. It's every single day. And there are probably software systems out there that make this easier. We just happen to be really hands-on.

teachings.explanation: Trades are expected to invoice only for work that has been completed, not in advance. Payment terms are fast once work is verified complete, reinforcing accountability and fairness. Scorecards for trades define expectations upfront and are developed over time through experience with pain points.
teachings.quote: If you invoice me, if you're a framer and you invoice me for a second floor and the first floor is the only thing up, like don't even try to invoice. Work is done. You get paid on Friday.

teachings.explanation: When building a project management team, owners should intentionally hire people with different strengths so they can support one another. One PM may excel at architectural detail, another at directness, another at financial flow. This creates a cross-functional team where PMs back each other up organically.
teachings.quote: I have someone who's super introspective, will sit with the detail forever. I have another one that will tackle the detail and recover. And then another one that understands money flow and understands architecture flow.

teachings.explanation: A shared video library in cloud storage where team members upload field discoveries, new techniques, and best practices as they are encountered. This keeps institutional knowledge growing and accessible to everyone on the team without waiting for formal training sessions.
teachings.quote: Every time we learn something in the field, it could be a waterproofing thing, it could be a new tile coming out, we share these things in this library so that people can access it and just keep learning.

teachings.explanation: Most situations that feel like emergencies in construction are not truly urgent. By having a structured calendar and requiring trades to compile their questions for scheduled site visits, owners and PMs can calm the chaos and prevent reactive decision-making that disrupts workflow.
teachings.quote: Construction feels urgent. Construction feels like an emergency, right? It feels like that. But when you actually look at your calendar and know when you're going to spend time in each place, if you're going to spend an hour there, the project manager is going to have a list of things that need to happen.

actionable_advice: Implement a project manager scorecard that defines non-negotiables including knowing the project better than anyone, submitting weekly reports, and recording daily videos per project.
Require project managers to record approximately three videos per day per project so ownership can review, catch issues, and stay informed without being on-site constantly.

Block out a fixed, recurring time slot on your calendar to visit each project site each week and communicate that schedule to all trades so they bring their questions to those times rather than interrupting you.
Set up a two-file invoice system: incoming invoices go to a holding file, and only PM-approved invoices move to an approved file for the money manager to process into draws.

Stagger client draw billing across the month, for example billing half of projects on the first and half on the fifteenth, to maintain consistent cashflow rather than having all draws go out simultaneously.
When vetting new subcontractors, ask your existing trades, material reps, and vendors for referrals, as the best tradespeople tend to have strong relationships with their suppliers.
After every project, conduct a financial debrief going through cost code by cost code to identify where estimates missed and update your pricing assumptions for future bids.
Build and maintain a shared cloud video library of field best practices and new techniques discovered on the job so the entire team has access to institutional knowledge.
When a trade sends an unexpected or unapproved invoice, get on the phone immediately rather than relying on email or text to resolve it quickly and clearly.
If a trade overspends without authorization and expects you to absorb the cost, require them to sit in the client meeting and explain the overage themselves rather than billing it through silently.
client_challenges: Steven's company is running seven active projects simultaneously with only three people sharing all roles, creating overwhelm and unclear ownership of responsibilities.
Abby's husband, the general contractor, is reluctant to hand off financial management because designs and costs are still being determined mid-project rather than defined upfront.
Linda's company has project managers handling four to five projects at a time in the $1M-$2M range and is struggling to create SOPs that keep PMs organized without slowing down operations.
Lauren is working with high-end artisan subcontractors who over-build and incur unexpected material costs, making it difficult to hold them to budgets without risking the relationship.
Bennett faced a competitive bid situation where structural unknowns required investigatory work before pricing could be confirmed, making it hard to present a confident and complete proposal.
A participant asked how to manage the pipeline so projects do not all hit simultaneously or leave gaps between jobs, which Erin acknowledged is largely unpredictable.
Renee was unclear whether the trade scorecard policy meant no draws at all for subcontractors or that draws must align with verified completion milestones.
notable_quotes: On project ownership: 'Architect didn't catch it. Designer didn't catch it. Engineer didn't catch it. Civil didn't catch it. That's what it means to own a project.'
On calendar discipline during hardship: 'We put our dog to sleep. That was with us for 14 years. Did my calendar change at all, Sabrina, other than that little chunk of time? No, it did not.'
On how the trade scorecard was developed: 'Pain. Like literally, pain points.'
On hiring people you'd want to work for: 'The culture is so important. I would totally work for everyone in our company. So you get to that, right?'

On construction urgency: 'Construction feels urgent. Construction feels like an emergency, right? It feels like that. But when you actually look at your calendar and know when you're going to spend time in each place, the project manager is going to have a list of things that need to happen.'

On not chasing clients: 'I'm not going to keep chasing this one.' — Bennett, describing his discipline in following up only twice before stepping back, which ultimately led the client to call him back two weeks later ready to move forward.
